text:
Most "CRM problems" aren't CRM problems.

They're definition problems. Nobody agreed on what a qualified lead is. Or what "active customer" means. Or which date counts as the close date when a deal slips a quarter.

The tool just makes the disagreement visible.

Before you migrate, re-platform, or hire a consultant — sit your sales and marketing heads in a room and ask them to define five terms. You'll learn more in 30 minutes than in a month of dashboards.

A CRM with 400 custom properties is not a more powerful CRM.

It's a slower one. Reps stop filling in fields they don't understand. Reports break because three properties mean the same thing. New hires need a week to learn the data model.

I've never audited a HubSpot or Salesforce instance where the answer was "you need more fields." It's always the opposite.

Sat down with a sales team last month that was convinced their pipeline reports were broken.

They weren't. The reports were fine. The problem was that two reps were moving deals to "Closed Won" the moment a verbal yes came in, and three others were waiting for the signed contract. Same stage. Two completely different meanings.

Took us 20 minutes to find it. Took them six months of arguing about forecast accuracy to admit something was off.

If your team doesn't trust the numbers, the first place to look isn't the dashboard. It's the stage definitions.

A simple CRM health check I run before touching anything:

1. Pull the last 90 days of closed deals. How many are missing a close reason?
2. Pull all open deals over 90 days old. Why are they still open?
3. Pull all contacts created in the last 30 days. What % have a lifecycle stage that makes sense?
4. Look at your top 3 workflows. When were they last edited? By whom?
5. Count your active properties. Now count the ones used in a report or workflow in the last year.

The gap between those last two numbers is usually where the real work is.

Happy to walk through this on a real instance if anyone wants a second set of eyes — DM me.

The most underrated CRM skill is saying no to a stakeholder who wants "just one more field."

Every field has a maintenance cost. Every field is a place where data can go stale, get mistyped, or contradict another field. The default answer should be "what existing property can we use" — not "let's add one."

If your portal has 300 properties and you can't remember why most of them exist, DM me — pruning is most of what I do.

A deal isn't a relationship with one person. It's a relationship with a committee.

Most CRMs let you tag contact roles on a deal — champion, economic buyer, technical evaluator, blocker. Almost nobody fills them in. Then the champion leaves, the deal stalls, and nobody knows who else to call.

Map the buying committee on every deal over a certain size. When someone goes dark, you'll have a backup. When you win, you'll know who actually drove it.

If your deals live and die on a single contact, DM me — mapping the committee is one of the highest-leverage habits a sales team can build.

Win/loss analysis is the most skipped high-value activity in sales.

Everyone's too busy chasing the next deal to study the last one. So the same losing patterns repeat for years. The same objection sinks deal after deal and nobody connects the dots.

You don't need a fancy program. A required two-field note on every closed deal — 'what actually drove this' and 'what would have changed it' — gives you a quarterly review worth more than most sales training.

If you close deals but never study why, send me a DM — the lightweight version of this is a one-day setup.

Bad CRM data isn't a data problem. It's an incentive problem.

If reps don't see value in updating a field, they won't. Threats won't fix it. Mandatory fields just produce garbage data faster. The fix is making the data useful to the person entering it — sales sequences that pull from notes, deal views that surface the next action, dashboards that reward thoroughness.

The reps who keep the cleanest data are the ones whose own workflow depends on it.

If you're trying to fix data hygiene with mandates and it isn't sticking, DM me — the incentive redesign is usually the actual fix.

Unpopular opinion: most companies don't need a deduplication tool. They need a deduplication process.

Tools find duplicates. They don't tell you which record to keep, which fields to merge, what to do with associated deals and tickets, or how to prevent the next one. Without a process, you just create duplicates faster than the tool can clean them.

Start with prevention rules — required fields, validation on entry, controlled import paths. The cleanup tool is the last 10%, not the first 90%.

If duplicates keep coming back faster than you can clean them, send me a DM — happy to walk through the prevention rules I usually start with.

A client had ~30k contacts with no first or last name — just "info@" addresses that got created from a years-old web form.

They couldn't run personalized sequences. They couldn't segment for events. Their email deliverability was suffering because half the list was junk.

The fix wasn't fancy: a one-time enrichment pass, a workflow to flag any new contact with no name within 7 days, and a re-engagement campaign that quietly suppressed anyone who didn't respond.

Open rate on their next campaign jumped 18%. Most of it was just removing the dead weight.

This kind of cleanup is straightforward but tedious — if you're staring at a list like this and not sure where to start, I'm happy to share the playbook. Just message me.

Three properties every B2B CRM should have, and most don't:

- Source of truth for company size (not "we have three different ones from three integrations")
- A single canonical owner field that drives routing, reporting, and notifications
- A reliable "last meaningful activity" timestamp that isn't fooled by automated emails

If you don't have these, every report downstream is approximate at best.

If you're not sure which of your fields is the real source of truth for company size or owner, DM me — that's usually the first thing I sort out on a new engagement.

The fastest way to improve CRM data quality isn't a tool. It's a Friday afternoon report that emails the sales manager a list of every deal closed that week with missing key fields.

Suddenly the fields get filled in. Funny how that works.

If your reps treat data entry as optional, message me — there are a handful of low-drama accountability loops that change behavior fast.

Data enrichment tools are sold as a fix for bad data. They're not. They're a fix for missing data.

If your records are wrong, enrichment overwrites them with someone else's guess. If your records are empty, enrichment fills useful gaps. Know which problem you have before you buy.

The teams that get value from enrichment turn it on for specific fields (company size, industry, tech stack) and leave the rest alone. The teams that don't let it overwrite everything and then can't trust any field.

If you're weighing an enrichment tool and not sure it'll help, DM me — happy to talk through what it can and can't fix.

'Single view of the customer' is the most expensive phrase in B2B software.

It sounds achievable. It rarely is. The data lives in five systems with five owners and five definitions, and unifying it perfectly is a multi-year project that's obsolete before it ships.

What actually works: pick the three or four fields people genuinely need in one place, sync those reliably, and stop. A 'good-enough view of the customer' beats a perfect one that never arrives.

If you're staring down a 'single view' project that keeps growing, send me a DM — scoping it down is usually the win.

Watched a sales ops lead spend two hours every Monday morning building the same pipeline report by hand. Pulling CSVs, pivoting, pasting into Slack.

When I asked why, the answer was: "the automated one doesn't have the columns leadership wants."

Took 40 minutes to rebuild the automated one with the right columns. That's 100 hours a year back.

Most "we need new software" conversations end this way. The tool already does it. Someone just never had the time to set it up properly.

If someone on your team is rebuilding the same report every Monday, DM me — the automated version is usually 40 minutes away.

Before you build a workflow, write the sentence:

"When [trigger], do [action], unless [exception], and notify [person] if [condition]."

If you can't write that sentence cleanly, the workflow isn't ready to be built. You'll discover the edge cases six weeks later when leads are being routed to the wrong rep and nobody can figure out why.

Workflows fail in the design, not the build.

If you're about to build a workflow and the spec is fuzzy, send me a DM — happy to pressure-test it before it ships.

The CRM workflows that quietly cause the most damage are the ones nobody remembers building.

Two years ago someone set up a lifecycle stage automation for a campaign that's long over. It's still firing. It's still overriding manual updates from reps. Nobody knows it exists because the person who built it left the company.

Once a quarter, open the automation panel and sort by "last modified" ascending. Read the bottom of that list. You will find at least one thing that shouldn't be running.

If you'd like a hand auditing your automations, I do this kind of cleanup for HubSpot teams — message me and I'll send over how I usually structure it.

A good CRM automation does one thing.

A bad CRM automation does seven things, has four branches, depends on a property that was renamed last year, and only runs on the third Tuesday of the month if the contact's owner is in EMEA.

When in doubt, build two workflows instead of one. Future-you will thank present-you.

If you've inherited a workflow that does seven things and you're afraid to touch it, DM me — I do this kind of untangling a lot.

The phrase "we'll just handle that in the CRM" has caused more downstream pain than almost any other sentence in modern B2B operations.

Usually said in a meeting where nobody from ops is present.

If ops keeps getting handed problems that were designed without them, DM me — I help teams build the muscle to push back earlier.

Your marketing emails are landing in spam and you're blaming the copy. It's probably the domain.

Deliverability is mostly technical hygiene: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC set up correctly, a warmed-up sending domain, and a list that isn't full of dead addresses. Get those wrong and the best subject line in the world lands in a folder nobody opens.

Check your authentication records before you rewrite another email. The fix is usually a config change, not a creative one.

If your open rates are quietly declining, DM me — deliverability audits are straightforward and the ROI is immediate.

The best activity logging is the kind reps don't have to do.

Modern CRMs can auto-capture emails and meetings from connected inboxes and calendars. Turn it on. The data you get from automatic capture is more complete and more honest than anything reps log by hand under deadline pressure.

Manual logging should be reserved for the things a machine can't see — the content of a call, the read on a stakeholder, the next step. Let the system handle the rest.

If your reps are spending real time on data entry that could be automated, send me a DM.

The dashboard nobody uses is the one that takes 10 seconds to load and shows 14 charts.

The dashboard everyone uses shows three numbers. Pipeline created this week. Deals stuck more than 30 days in their current stage. Activities logged per rep.

Less is more, and it's not even close.

If your dashboards are 14 charts deep and nobody opens them, send me a DM — the rebuild is usually a half-day, not a quarter.

A sales leader told me her team "wouldn't adopt the CRM."

Sat with three reps for an hour each. Two of them had open tabs to spreadsheets where they were tracking their own pipeline because the CRM views were unusable. The third was logging activities in a personal notebook because she'd been burned by a sync issue years ago.

These were not adoption problems. These were UX problems and trust problems. Once we rebuilt their views around how they actually worked, adoption took care of itself.

If your team is "resisting the CRM," the most useful thing you can do is shadow three of them for a day. The reason is always in there.

If you're hearing 'the team won't adopt the CRM,' DM me — the view rebuild that fixes it is usually less work than people expect.

The methodical way to improve CRM performance, in one paragraph:

Audit your data model and kill what you don't use. Define your terms and make sure sales and marketing agree. Fix data entry incentives before you buy enrichment tools. Document every automation and retire the dead ones quarterly. Build reports that answer one question each. Then — and only then — start adding new capabilities.

It's not glamorous. It's not a re-platform. But it's the work that compounds.

This is most of what I do for teams on the side. If your HubSpot (or whatever you're on) feels heavier than it should, send me a message — happy to take a look.

Reporting tip that has saved me more meetings than I can count:

For every recurring report you publish, add a "what changed since last time" line at the top. Not "pipeline is $4.2M." Instead: "Pipeline is $4.2M, up $300k from last week, driven by three new enterprise deals from the partnerships channel."

People don't want numbers. They want the story the numbers tell.

If your reporting is numbers without narrative and leadership keeps asking 'so what,' send me a DM — happy to share the templates I use.

A month of posts on improving CRM performance, and the takeaway I keep coming back to:

The teams that get the most out of their CRM aren't the ones with the biggest tech stack or the fanciest setup. They're the ones who treat it as a living system — pruned regularly, well-understood by the people who use it, and tied to decisions that actually get made.

Tools don't fix culture. But a thoughtful, well-maintained CRM removes the friction that makes good culture hard to sustain.

If any of these posts hit a nerve and you're staring at a CRM that's grown more than it's been gardened, I help teams (mostly on HubSpot) work through exactly this. Send me a DM — even if it's just to compare notes.

Everyone worries about duplicate contacts. The quieter problem is duplicate companies.

'Acme Inc,' 'Acme, Inc.,' 'Acme Incorporated,' and 'acme.com' all as separate company records. Now your account-based reporting is wrong, your territory assignments overlap, and two reps are working the same logo without knowing it.

Company dedup is harder than contact dedup because the matching signals are weaker. Domain is your best key — normalize on it ruthlessly.

If your account list has the same company three times under different spellings, DM me — happy to share how I approach company-level dedup.

The number every sales leader should know and most can't recite: pipeline coverage ratio.

Open pipeline for the quarter divided by the quota for the quarter. Under 3x and you're almost certainly going to miss — there isn't enough in the funnel to absorb normal loss rates. Over 5x and either you're sandbagging or your pipeline is full of deals that aren't real.

If your CRM can't show you this number in one click, that's the first report to build. Everything about forecast confidence flows from it.

If you don't have coverage ratio at your fingertips, send me a DM — it's a 20-minute build.

Your CRM says the customer is on the Pro plan. Your billing system says Starter. Your CS platform says Enterprise.

Which one's right? Usually nobody knows, because there's no agreed-on source of truth — there's just whichever system the person asking the question logged into first.

Pick one. Make every other system point at it. Until you do, every report is a coin flip.

If your CRM, billing, and CS tools disagree on basic customer facts, DM me — picking a source of truth is the cheapest fix you can make this quarter.

A sales manager once told me he didn't trust his CRM dashboard "because it doesn't match what my reps tell me on Friday calls."

I asked which one he trusted more. He said his reps.

Then why have the dashboard.

Took a month to figure out the dashboard was actually right and the reps were doing optimistic math out loud. Once everyone trusted the data, the Friday calls got shorter and the forecasts got more accurate.

The CRM is only useful if leadership treats it as the source of truth. The day leadership starts second-guessing it, the reps stop maintaining it.

If your leadership team second-guesses the CRM on every forecast call, send me a DM — the trust rebuild starts with one fix at a time.

Lead scoring rules that actually work:

1. Score on behavior, not demographics. A VP who downloaded one whitepaper is less qualified than a Manager who opened three emails and visited the pricing page twice.

2. Decay scores. If someone hit 80 six months ago and hasn't done anything since, they aren't an 80 anymore. Most scoring models forget this.

3. Have a "why" next to every rule. If the rule is "+10 for opening 3 emails," write down why three is the threshold. If you can't, lower it.

4. Get sales to sign off. If sales doesn't trust the score, the score is useless.

Lead scoring fails in half the companies I see, almost always for one of these four reasons. Happy to look at yours if you want a sanity check — DM me.

The hardest thing about CRM admin isn't building anything new.

It's deciding what to retire.

If you've got a backlog of 'we should probably retire that' items, DM me — I run a quarterly kill-list pass for HubSpot teams.

Custom objects are usually a mistake.

Yes, your business has "projects" or "subscriptions" or "units" that don't fit neatly into Contacts/Companies/Deals. And yes, your CRM lets you model them.

But every custom object adds: a new permission matrix, a new reporting surface, new sync logic with downstream systems, new training for reps, new edge cases your automations don't handle.

Nine times out of ten you can solve the same problem with a well-structured property on the parent object plus a tag. The tenth time genuinely needs a custom object — and you'll know because the "just use a property" workaround keeps breaking.

If you're weighing a custom object and not sure it's worth the maintenance tax, send me a DM — happy to talk it through before you commit.

Your contact database has a hoarding problem.

Contacts from a trade show in 2019. Leads who never replied. People who left the companies you sold to three jobs ago. They inflate your numbers, hurt your deliverability, and make every list slower to build.

Archiving isn't deleting — it's moving the dead weight out of the active working set. A quarterly archive of contacts with zero engagement in 18+ months keeps the database honest.

If your contact count keeps climbing but engagement keeps falling, DM me — the archive process is a one-time setup with permanent payoff.

If you sell software, your CRM is missing your most predictive data: product usage.

A contact who logs in daily and invites teammates is a different sales conversation than one who signed up and vanished. Yet most sales teams work off form fills and email opens while the usage data sits in a separate system nobody connects.

Pipe a few key usage signals into the CRM — last login, active seats, feature adoption — and your reps suddenly know who's actually worth a call.

If your sales team is flying blind on product usage, send me a DM — wiring in the key signals changes everything about prioritization.

Watched a team spend three months building a "unified customer view" dashboard.

It pulled from CRM, billing, support, product, marketing automation. Sixteen widgets. Color-coded health bars. Beautiful.

Nobody used it.

Why? Because the people who needed customer information already had a Slack channel where they'd ping each other with "hey, what's going on with Acme?" That workflow had 100% adoption, near-zero latency, and the conversation history was searchable.

The dashboard solved a problem nobody had. The Slack channel was working.

Before you build anything, ask: how are people solving this without my tool? Sometimes the answer is "not very well." Sometimes it's "actually, fine." The second one is more common than you'd think.

If you're about to build something big to solve a problem people are already working around, DM me — sometimes the answer is 'leave it alone.'

The four-question gut check before any CRM project:

1. Who specifically asked for this? (Not "leadership" — a name.)
2. What decision will they make with the output? (If they can't answer, it's vanity work.)
3. What happens if I build the simplest version first and stop there?
4. Who owns it after I'm done? (If nobody, it'll rot.)

I've killed more half-finished projects with question 4 than the other three combined.

If you've got a CRM project that's stalled or that keeps getting deprioritized, DM me — happy to run it through these four questions with you.

Your CRM is a tool, not a strategy. Your reporting is a tool, not a strategy. Your AI agent is a tool, not a strategy.

You still need to decide who you're going after, what you're selling them, and how. No software makes that decision for you. Most software pretends it can, and then you spend a year debugging why it didn't.

If you're between platforms or stacks and feel like more tooling will fix things, send me a DM — happy to talk through what the tooling can and can't do for you.

A founder asked me last quarter what's the one CRM change that would 10x his sales team's productivity.

I said: "There isn't one."

He looked frustrated. So I added: there are about 15 small changes — each worth maybe 5–10% — that add up to a significant improvement over six months. None of them are sexy. None of them look impressive in a board deck. But together they're the difference between a CRM that works and a CRM that gets abandoned for spreadsheets.

The companies that get this right treat CRM optimization as a continuous practice, not a project. The ones that don't keep replatforming every two years and wondering why nothing changes.

If you want a list of the 15 small changes I usually start with on a HubSpot portal, DM me — I'll send it over.

Six weeks of posts on CRM performance, and the thing I keep coming back to:

The teams that get this right treat their CRM like a garden — not a one-time install. Continuous pruning. Continuous re-planting. Definitions that drift get redefined. Workflows that age get retired. Reports that nobody reads get killed.

The teams that get it wrong treat it like a renovation project. Big push every 18 months. Replatform. Migrate. Retrain. Then it slowly rots until the next replatform.

If you're staring at a CRM that feels like it's drifting toward the second pattern, send me a DM. The fix isn't a tool — it's a cadence.

The worst moment in most customer relationships is the handoff from sales to CS.

The rep who spent two months learning the account disappears. The CS manager inherits a closed-won deal with no context — no notes on why they bought, what was promised, who the real users are. The customer re-explains everything and quietly loses confidence.

A structured handoff in the CRM — required fields at close that capture goals, promises, and key contacts — turns that cliff into a bridge.

If your sales-to-CS handoff loses context every time, DM me — the fix is a short checklist enforced at the close stage.

Unmanaged discounting is a margin leak hiding in your CRM.

When every rep can apply any discount to close a deal, two things happen: margins erode quietly, and customers learn that your price is fiction. The deal that closed at 40% off trains the next buyer to demand the same.

Build discount approval into the deal workflow — anything past a threshold routes to a manager. Not to slow deals down, but to make the exception visible and deliberate.

If discounting on your team is a free-for-all, send me a DM — governance workflows are quick to build and pay for themselves fast.

If you can't explain in one sentence why a custom property exists, delete it.

"We might need it someday" is not an explanation. Neither is "Joe in finance asked for it in 2022."

Every dead property makes the live ones harder to trust. Reps stop reading field labels. Reports quietly pull from the wrong one. New hires waste their first week learning which fields are real.

Adding fields is easy. Deleting is political. The fix is a recurring 30-minute "kill list" meeting — the team picks one property to remove, every two weeks, no exceptions. After a quarter you'll wonder how the instance ever got that bloated.

If your property list has gotten ahead of you, DM me — the kill-list meeting is one of the higher-leverage things a CRM team can do.

The most useful CRM metric nobody tracks: 'time in current stage.'

Deal age tells you how long the deal has existed. Time in current stage tells you whether momentum has stalled. A 90-day-old deal in Negotiation is healthy. A 90-day-old deal stuck in Discovery for 60 of those days is dying.

Build the report. Sort it. Look at the top 10. Those are the deals your team is lying to themselves about.

If you can't easily build this report in your CRM today, DM me — it's usually a 30-minute fix that changes how forecast calls go.

'Lost - Price' is not a lost reason. It's a shrug.

The actual lost reasons that help you change behavior look like: 'lost to incumbent because we couldn't prove ROI in their evaluation window,' 'lost on security review,' 'champion left mid-cycle,' 'budget cycle slipped.'

If your lost-reason picklist has four generic options and one of them is 'other,' you're collecting data for a report nobody reads.

If your lost-reason data is too vague to be useful, send me a DM — rebuilding this picklist is one of the highest-ROI hours your RevOps team can spend.

Most companies discover the cost of mandatory fields the hard way.

Reps figure out the workaround within a week. They type 'TBD' in every text field. They pick the first dropdown option for every required dropdown. The fields are filled in. The data is worthless.

Mandatory fields work for two things: enforcing core record integrity (every contact has an email) and capturing decision-quality data at a moment the rep is naturally engaged (close reason at the moment of close). Everything else just teaches reps to game your CRM.

If your required fields are producing garbage data and you're not sure which to keep, DM me — happy to walk through how to triage them.

Ask five people at your company what an 'active customer' is. You'll get six answers.

Marketing says 'opened an email in the last 90 days.' Sales says 'on a paid plan with usage above zero.' CS says 'logged in within the trailing month.' Finance says 'invoiced last quarter.' Product says 'has at least one active seat.'

Every definition is reasonable. None of them match each other. Your dashboards have been quietly disagreeing for years.

If you've never sat down to define 'active customer' across teams, send me a DM — this is the cheapest hour of alignment you'll get all quarter.

The longest-running argument in B2B: was that deal marketing-sourced or sales-sourced?

Marketing claims the lead. Sales claims the relationship. Both are partly right, and the fight wastes more energy than the attribution is worth.

The healthiest teams stop trying to assign single-source credit and adopt a simple shared model — first touch and last touch both recorded, both visible, neither team's bonus riding on a coin flip. The CRM should inform the conversation, not referee a turf war.

If attribution fights are eating your team's energy, DM me — a shared model defuses most of it.

'The VP asked for a report' is how most CRM clutter begins.

A one-time question becomes a permanent dashboard. The VP looks at it twice and forgets it. Six months later it's still auto-refreshing, still maintained, still cited in meetings by people who don't know it's stale.

Before you build a report, ask: is this a question or a system? Questions get answered once. Systems get built and owned. Confusing the two is how you end up with 80 dashboards and trust in none.

If your reporting has metastasized from one-off requests, send me a DM — the cleanup is mostly about saying no going forward.

The sales-marketing SLA most teams sign and forget:

'Marketing will pass MQLs within 24 hours of qualification. Sales will action MQLs within 24 hours of receipt. Either side can dispute the qualification within 48 hours.'

Nice document. Lives in Notion. Doesn't exist in the CRM.

The version that actually works: a workflow that timestamps the MQL pass, alerts the sales manager when the 24-hour SLA breaches, and reports breach rates weekly. Now you have an SLA. Before, you had a hope.

If your SLAs live in docs and not in your CRM, DM me — wiring them in is usually a half-day's work.

Changing a deal owner in your CRM does more damage than people realize.

Most CRMs overwrite the owner field without preserving history. Six months later you're trying to answer 'who closed this deal' and the answer is 'whoever owns it now,' which is wrong.

The fix is a workflow that copies the old owner to a 'previous owner' field on any change, and stamps a 'last owner change date.' Your historical reporting survives reorgs, churn, and territory shifts.

If your historical pipeline reports look weird after a reorg, send me a DM — owner-change tracking is usually the missing piece.

Calculated properties are the most underused feature in HubSpot.

Examples of things I've solved with them in the last year: 'is this deal at risk' (true if stage age > 30 days AND no activity in 14 days), 'company tier' (based on revenue, employee count, deal history), 'customer lifecycle in months' (today minus first close date).

The alternative — a workflow that updates a property on a schedule — drifts the moment the underlying data changes. A calculated property is always right. It's also slower to query at scale, which is the tradeoff.

If you have workflows updating properties that could be a calculated property instead, DM me — happy to walk through which is right for what.

What I look at when a new sales hire isn't ramping in the CRM:

Day 30: are they logging activities, or just doing them? If activities aren't logged, the manager has no shot at coaching.

Day 60: are their deals moving through stages, or piling up in early discovery? Pile-ups usually mean qualification training never landed.

Day 90: is their pipeline-to-quota ratio in a normal range for the role? If not, the input mix is wrong — too few accounts, wrong ICP fit, or unrealistic conversion expectations.

These three checks beat any onboarding survey. The data tells you what coaching the rep actually needs.

If you don't have a structured way to read new-hire CRM data for coaching signals, send me a DM — happy to share the framework.

Every CRM has tech debt. Most teams pretend it doesn't.

Then the rebuild happens. And the rebuild turns up workflows nobody understands, properties nobody owns, integrations nobody can map. Six months and three-hundred-thousand dollars later, the rebuild ships and everyone swears they'll keep the new instance clean.

Eighteen months after that, it looks exactly like the old one.

The way out is treating CRM as continuous practice, not project. A small amount of cleanup, every quarter, forever.

If your CRM is heading toward 'we need to rebuild' territory, DM me — the maintenance practice is cheaper than the rebuild every time.

Selling internationally turns your clean CRM into a mess of edge cases.

Phone numbers in twelve formats. Dates as MM/DD here and DD/MM there. Company suffixes — GmbH, Ltd, SARL, Pty — that break your dedup matching. Currencies that need a single reporting standard. Address fields that assume US states.

None of it is hard individually. All of it compounds if you don't standardize on entry. Normalize formats at the point of import, not in cleanup six months later.

If your international data is a formatting swamp, DM me — happy to share the normalization rules I start with.

'Is the team actually using the CRM?' is a measurable question. Most leaders treat it as a vibe.

The signals are right there: percentage of deals with a next step and a date, activities logged per rep per week, percentage of contacts with complete core fields, time between stage changes. Trend those four and you know exactly where adoption is real and where it's theater.

You can't improve adoption you don't measure. And 'it feels like people aren't using it' is not a metric.

If you want a simple CRM adoption scorecard for your team, send me a DM — it's a half-day to set up and it changes the conversation.

The forecast call your reps dread is a CRM data problem in disguise.

If reps can update their deal stage right before the call, your forecast is performative. If stage definitions are fuzzy, every rep is forecasting on a different scale. If the system of record is 'whatever the rep said on Friday,' the dashboard is theater.

Real forecast hygiene: stage changes tracked with timestamps, definitions documented and tested quarterly, a freeze on commit-stage changes 48 hours before the call. Boring. Effective.

If your forecast accuracy is unreliable and you suspect the CRM data is the cause, DM me — happy to audit how your stages are actually being used.

Modeling a multi-product company in your CRM is harder than it looks.

The question is always: are these one deal with line items, or separate deals? The answer is almost always 'it depends on the sales motion.' If they're sold together, one deal. If they're sold separately to the same buyer at different times, separate deals. If they're cross-sells from CS to existing accounts, almost always separate deals.

Get this wrong and your pipeline reports double-count or under-count for years. The schema decision is structural — once you pick, changing it is brutal.

If you're growing into a multi-product motion and not sure how to model it, send me a DM — this is the conversation worth having before, not after.

Renewals are not a sales funnel. They're a customer success funnel that happens to involve revenue.

Most teams cram renewals into the new-business pipeline anyway. They use the same stages. They use the same close-date logic. They get useless reporting because a renewal that lands on time is not the same event as a new deal that closes after a competitive process.

A separate pipeline for renewals — even just for reporting — clarifies more than any other single CRM change I recommend to growth-stage companies.

If your renewals and new business are colliding in the same pipeline, DM me — splitting them is a one-week project with multi-year payoff.

Open your CRM's permissions panel. How many roles do you have. How many of them are 'X but slightly different.'

Permissions debt is the quietest tech debt. Nobody notices it until someone leaves the company and you spend a week figuring out which records they could see. Or until the audit comes and you can't explain why marketing has edit access to closed-won opportunities.

A quarterly review of who-can-see-what is not glamorous. It's also the single biggest predictor of whether your CRM will pass a SOC 2 audit on the first try.

If your permissions structure has grown organically for years, send me a DM — happy to walk through how to clean it up without breaking workflows.

If your CRM sends reps 40 notifications a day, it sends them zero. They've muted the tab.

Notification fatigue is self-inflicted. Every 'a contact opened your email' alert trains people to ignore the 'a deal worth $200k just went cold' alert sitting right next to it.

Audit your notifications like you'd audit properties. Keep the handful that demand action. Kill the rest, or route them to a digest nobody has to react to in real time.

The goal isn't more awareness. It's making the few alerts that matter impossible to miss.

If your team has muted CRM notifications entirely, DM me — pruning the noise is what gets the signal noticed again.

note: Watched a team spend 20 minutes in a meeting arguing about why the pipeline report was wrong. Turned out two people had different ideas of what 'closed' meant. The report was fine. The definitions weren't.
text:
Watched a team argue for 20 minutes about a broken pipeline report.

The report wasn't broken.

One person counted a deal as "closed" when the contract was signed. The other waited until payment cleared. Same stage, same property, two completely different mental models.

No dashboard fixes that. No new tool fixes that. You can rebuild the report in Looker, Tableau, or HubSpot's native dashboards and you'll get the same fight next quarter.

This is what I mean when I say most CRM problems are definition problems. The data is usually fine. The plumbing is usually fine. What's missing is a one-sentence, written-down answer to: what does this stage actually mean, and who decides when a deal enters it?

If you can't answer that for every stage in your pipeline, the next "the numbers look off" meeting is already on the calendar. You just don't know it yet.

What's the stage in your pipeline that two people on your team would define differently right now?
hook_options: Watched a team argue for 20 minutes about a broken pipeline report. The report wasn't broken.
Two people. Two definitions of 'closed.' One useless pipeline report.
Your pipeline report isn't wrong. Your team just doesn't agree on what 'closed' means.
20-minute meeting about a wrong pipeline report. The report was right.

note: Cleaned up a HubSpot portal today with 47 lifecycle stage values. Forty-seven. Half were typos or one-offs someone added for a single campaign in 2022 and never removed.
text:
47 lifecycle stage values in one HubSpot portal. That is not a CRM, that is a graveyard.

The breakdown was about what you'd expect:

- 6 actual stages anyone could define
- 4 typos of those 6 ("Custmer", "MQL " with a trailing space)
- 12 one-offs from 2022 campaigns nobody remembers
- The rest? Stages someone invented because the existing ones "didn't quite fit"

This is the part people miss when they blame the tool. HubSpot didn't do this. A series of small, reasonable-in-the-moment decisions did, made by people who never had to agree on what a customer actually is.

Before you buy a new platform, or build a new workflow, or hire a new ops person, try this: get sales, marketing, and CS in a room and ask them to define MQL. Out loud. Watch what happens.

The cleanup isn't the project. The agreement is. The cleanup is just what you do once the adults have decided.

What's the highest stage count you've inherited, and did anyone defend it with a straight face?

If you're staring at a portal like this and don't know where to start, my inbox is open.
hook_options: 47 lifecycle stage values in one HubSpot portal. That is not a CRM, that is a graveyard.
Cleaned up a portal today with 47 lifecycle stages. Half were typos.
Your CRM doesn't have a data problem. It has a definitions problem.
A lifecycle stage called 'MQL ' (with a trailing space) is not a stage. It's a crime scene.

note: A client asked me to build a complicated lead-scoring model. I asked when they last looked at their current one. Nobody could remember. We turned it off for a month and the sales numbers didn't move.
text:
We turned off their lead scoring model for a month. Sales didn't move.

A client asked me to build them a more sophisticated lead-scoring model.

First question I asked: when did you last look at the one you have?

Nobody in the room could answer.

So instead of building, we turned the existing model off. Thirty days. See what happens.

Same pipeline. Same close rate. Same everything.

That model wasn't filtering leads. It wasn't prioritizing rep effort. It was just making the CRM feel more serious than it was.

This happens more than people admit.

Lead scoring sounds rigorous. So teams build one, move on, and never check if it's actually connected to outcomes. The model sits there assigning scores, and nobody asks whether those scores change anything.

Before you add complexity, try the subtraction test. Turn it off. Wait 30 days.

If nothing changes, you didn't have a model. You had a number.

Has your sales team ever pushed back and said the scores feel meaningless — and what did you actually do with that?

If you want a second set of eyes on what you've got running, feel free to drop me a message.
hook_options: We turned off their lead scoring model for a month. Sales didn't move.
Nobody in the room could remember when they'd last looked at their lead scoring model. That told me everything I needed to know.
The best lead scoring audit I've ever run: turn it off and wait.
A client wanted a more sophisticated lead-scoring model. I suggested we try deleting the one they had first.

text:
Nobody noticed when the sync broke.

The report just looked a little off. Someone raised it in a meeting four
days later. By then, four days of pipeline data had drifted.

The fix was obvious in retrospect: a daily digest. Five numbers emailed
every morning.

Contacts created yesterday. Deals missing an amount. Lifecycle stage
distribution. Active workflow errors. Form submissions from the past 24
hours.

Not a dashboard. Not a BI tool. A boring email with five lines.

If a number moves in a way that doesn't make sense, you catch it the day it
happens — not when someone finally runs the report.

An unobserved CRM degrades silently. A daily digest is the cheapest
early-warning system in your stack.

Most portals I walk into have zero monitoring. Does yours have any?

If you want to set something like this up, drop me a message.
